title: Stability, non-approximated groups and high-dimensional expanders
speaker: Alexander Lubotzky
affiliation: Hebrew University of Jerusalem; Visiting Professor, School of Mathematics
abstract: Several well-known open questions, such as: "are all groups sofic or hyperlinear?", have a common form: can all groups be approximated by asymptotic homomorphisms into the symmetric groups Sym(n) (in the sofic case) or the unitary groups U(n) (in the hyperlinear case)? In the case of U(n), the question can be asked with respect to different metrics and norms. We answer, for the first time, some of these versions, showing that there exist finitely presented groups which are not approximated by U(n) with respect to the Frobenius (=L2) norm and many other norms.

proof_stages.label: Metric approximation framework for groups
proof_stages.summary: The talk builds from residual finiteness to soficity and then to the general notion of approximation by maps into metric groups that are asymptotically homomorphic and separate points.

proof_stages.label: Unitary norm hierarchy and non-approximation theorems
proof_stages.summary: For unitary groups with Hilbert--Schmidt, Frobenius, Schatten p, and operator norms, the speaker states the main results: finitely presented groups not Frobenius-approximated and one finitely presented group not p-approximated for every 1<p<infinity.

proof_stages.label: Stability reduction to residual finiteness
proof_stages.summary: Stability is defined as the property that every asymptotic homomorphism is close to a genuine homomorphism, yielding the key reduction that a finitely generated group that is both approximated and stable must be residually finite.

proof_stages.label: H^2 criterion for Frobenius stability
proof_stages.summary: A cohomological theorem is introduced: if H^2 of the group vanishes for every unitary Hilbert-space representation, then the group is stable for the Frobenius norm.

proof_stages.label: Garland theory and high-dimensional expanders supply H^2 vanishing
proof_stages.summary: Garland--Ballmann--Swiatkowski vanishing for cocompact lattices acting on high-rank p-adic buildings gives the needed H^2-vanishing, interpreting these lattices and their quotients as high-dimensional-expander sources.

proof_stages.label: Deligne-style non-residually finite central extensions
proof_stages.summary: A p-adic analogue of Deligne’s finite central extensions gives non-residually finite groups while preserving H^2 vanishing, so stability plus non-residual finiteness proves the Frobenius non-approximation theorem.

proof_stages.label: Ultraproduct lifting mechanism for stability
proof_stages.summary: The proof of the H^2 criterion is sketched by converting asymptotic homomorphisms into homomorphisms into a metric ultraproduct and identifying stability with a lifting problem through a kernel filtered by Hilbert-space extensions.

proof_stages.label: Schatten-p Banach extension and remaining open cases
proof_stages.summary: The Frobenius argument is extended to Schatten p norms, 1<p<infinity, by proving the required cohomology vanishing for corresponding Banach coefficients and using a universal finitely presented group, while the sofic, hyperlinear, p=1, and operator-norm cases are left as problems likely requiring nonabelian H^2 methods.
